title: Raffinose and glycerol co-modulation of reuterin production in fermented milk: Antimicrobial efficacy against Staphylococcus aureus and quality improvement.
author_string: Zhou, Mengyu, Hu, Yingsheng, He, Shihan, Zhao, Kui, Hu, Jieting et al.
abstract: Fermented milk is a nutrient-rich food with beneficial probiotic properties, yet its production faces challenges such as limited fermentation strain diversity and susceptibility to foodborne pathogens persist. This study aimed to enhance the production of the broad-spectrum antimicrobial reuterin by Limosilactobacillus reuteri HLRE05 in fermented milk through the addition of glycerol and raffinose. By optimizing the concentrations of these supplements, reuterin yield significantly increased to 0.70 ± 0.01 mg/mL. While the addition of reuterin altered the fermented milk properties, increasing the pH, reducing the water-holding capacity, and impairing the rheological quality in glycerol-only systems. However, raffinose supplementation counteracted these adverse effects, promoting acid production and curd formation. Remarkably, reuterin effectively inhibited Staphylococcus aureus growth and biofilm formation, and eliminated its contamination during fermentation and storage. Throughout storage, reuterin levels remained sufficient to prevent over-acidification and inhibit foodborne pathogens, with optimal water-holding and antioxidant capacities were observed on the seventh day. This study advances the development of eco-friendly food preservation strategies and highlights the application of probiotic-derived antimicrobials in enhancing food safety and quality.
